stories.title: The Cartographer's Spite
stories.text:
Aldwyn the mapmaker had been blind for three seasons before the Remaking shattered the world. Now, in a sun-scorched refuge camp north of Pale Crossing, his sightlessness was a gift. The Dominion auditors could not read the maps he carved into clay tablets using fingers that remembered every contour of the old world—maps that contradicted the state's phantom-ledgers. A young refugee girl brought him water and fragments of star-ash, the only currency left. "They're coming," she whispered. "The grey men with ledgers."

Aldwyn smiled. He had already hidden copies of his ninth map in twelve separate camps across the Ashen Reach, each one deliberately *incomplete*—missing crucial coordinates, routes that looped back on themselves, false prophecies written in the Elder Tongue syllables he'd learned from a dying bone-singer. If the Dominion sought to prevent the Tenth Ascension by completing a perfect map, he would ensure they found only labyrinths. When the auditors arrived at dusk, they found him at his work-stone, humming an old cartographer's song. One reached for his tablets. Aldwyn did not resist.

stories.title: The Deepkin Sentinel's Choice
stories.text: Captain Merenna had worn her reflection-echo like a second skin for so long that she could no longer separate her own will from its counsel. Both selves floated in the pearl-chamber of the Sapphire Deep, watching the Tear—the boundary-seal holding back the abyss—fracture like frost-split stone. The Council of Depths had given her three options: a traditional blood-sacrifice (five willing souls), a living covenant (renewed daily through choice), or a threshold-being mediation (impossible, suicidal, untested). Merenna's reflection moved toward the barrier. She felt it before the echo acted—a pulling, a recognition. They would merge. Not into one voice, but into a *bridge*: a consciousness straddling both sides of the seal, holding the waters apart through sheer will and love. No deepkin had ever survived such binding. But as her echo pressed forward, Merenna understood: the Tear was not a lock. It was a covenant, and covenants could only be renewed by sacrifice *and* choice—and she was choosing this.

stories.title: The Goblin-Locksmith's Silver Tongue
stories.text:
Grethuk the Goblin-King had always been a thief before he was royalty. When three human merchant-lords arrived at his throne-chamber beneath the Spire of Blackstone demanding compensation for "stolen trade routes," he grinned with all his teeth. The vault-lord, a scarred dwarf named Korgin Ironfoot, had been his rival for forty years. The tax-collector from the Hearthlands wanted tariffs on goblin-worked silver. The spice-merchant simply wanted *theft* to be illegal again, so profit could matter. Grethuk offered them a wager instead of a contract. "In three days," he said, "I will steal something from each of you. Something precious. If I succeed, the Dominion's ledgers mean nothing here—we make our own law. If I fail, you take whatever compensation you wish."

The merchant-lords laughed. The King of Locks couldn't possibly—

On the first night, Grethuk stole Korgin's most famous blade by replacing it with an indistinguishable copy he'd forged thirty years prior, hidden in the throne-chamber since before the Remaking.

stories.title: The Priestess of Vesh's Last Covenant
stories.text:
The temple of Vesh in Pelimor's High Quarter had not seen its god answer a prayer since the Remaking. The priestess, Sorenne, had spent seventy-three days in the stone chapel without sleep, carving covenant-marks into her own skin with a blessed knife, singing the old water-songs her grandmother had forbidden her to learn. Tonight, Vesh would return—or the priestess would break the seal and damn them all. A young soldier entered the chapel, dragging a prisoner. "The auditors want him dead," the soldier said quietly. "For resisting ledger-work. I brought him to you because..."

Because in a world gone mad, temples were the only places left that offered choices beyond survival. Sorenne looked at the prisoner—a boy, really, no more than twenty, marked with the scars of furnace-work. She looked at the soldier. Then she picked up the knife. She carved not into her own flesh, but into the temple's foundation stones, writing the prisoner's name in the Elder Tongue syllables. She sang it aloud.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Winter Gift
stories.text: Velka the wyvern had not descended from her peak in the Ironspine Mountains for three hundred years. Gold was all she needed—gold and the memory of gold, and the memory of blood, and the memory of conquest. But the Remaking had turned her hoard to dust, and the dust sang with voices of the refugees she'd ignored for centuries. She found them huddled at the mountain's eastern slope: a caravan trapped by early snow, their supplies exhausted, a child already cold-sick. Velka could have left them. Should have. Instead, she folded her great wings around the camp—not to crush, but to shelter. Her scales radiated heat older than the sun. As the refugees settled beneath her, drinking in warmth, she felt something crack open in her chest. Not softness. Not mercy, exactly. But recognition: the child had no more value than gold dust now, and yet the child's life felt more real than any coin ever had. In the morning, the snow had cleared. Velka stood sentinel as they packed to leave. The caravan-master approached, trembling.

stories.title: The Rust-Thief's Gambit
stories.text: Merin Coldwater had one advantage: the Dominion didn't believe a glassblower could steal. She moved through Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter at dusk, when the furnace-workers clocked out and the auditors rotated shifts. The vault beneath the administrative heart lay seven floors down, guarded by ledger-locks that sang in frequencies only the Dominion's trained ears could parse. But Merin had spent three months learning to hum in the Elder Tongue—a frequency that made ledger-magic hesitate, just long enough. She descended the spiral stairs, her breath shallow. The consciousness-fragments kept in that vault weren't treasure to her. They were people: scattered identities, stolen moments of joy, memories of loved ones the Remaking had scattered across impossible distances. The Dominion called them currency. Merin called them ransom. She reached the lowest chamber. The vault door shimmered with phantom-script, a Dominion invention that rewrote itself every candleflash to confound thieves. Merin didn't pick it. Instead, she withdrew a bone-flute carved by Mesek himself—payment for which she'd surrendered three years of her remaining life. The flute sang.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Threshold
stories.text: Skarath had guarded the Ironspine caverns for three centuries, accumulating gold until the Remaking turned it to ash. Now she watched refugees shelter in the caves her hoard had once occupied, and something in her ancient heart shifted. The deepkin delegation arrived at dusk—three commanders glowing with bioluminescence, bearing a proposal that would reshape the boundary-waters accord. They sought an ancient dragon's witness to a new covenant: one that required daily choice instead of blood-sacrifice to maintain the Tear of the Drowned God. Skarath listened from her perch above, scales corroded with rust-patina that would not scrub away. The lead deepkin captain, Merith, spoke with the cadence of someone who had already died once and been reshaped by it. "A dragon's testimony carries weight in the old laws," Merith said. "We need a voice older than the Dominion, older than the Luminous Ascension." Skarath descended, her massive frame casting shadows that eclipsed the refugees huddled below. She had spoken to no one in seven years. When her voice emerged, it carried the rasp of stone grinding stone.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Daughter
stories.text: Thessa had learned locks from her father's hands before the Remaking scattered him across three incompatible timelines. Now, alone in Keshtir's merchant quarter, she held his final gift: a key of meteoric iron and bone-resonance, carved to open not doors but possibilities. Thessa recognized the ledger-brand on her palm—forced service, no choice. "I can't let you pass," Thessa said quietly. The auditor's face hardened. "Then I'll burn the waystation and everyone in it." Thessa had trained for this. She placed her father's key in the auditor's palm and spoke the only words of the Elder Tongue she truly understood: "Choose again." The key burned against the auditor's skin, not with heat but with memory. For a moment, the woman stood suspended between the person she'd been before the Dominion conscripted her and the enforcer she'd become. Thessa saw her father in that suspended instant—caught in the same pause, the same impossible choice. The auditor dropped the key and walked away. Thessa never learned which version of herself she'd chosen to be.

stories.title: The Spice-Weaver's Reckoning
stories.text: Raen had been a merchant's daughter before she became a spice-runner, and a threshold-being after the Remaking began to transform her. Half her body existed in the world's current iteration; the other half flickered between versions, aging and reversing in turns. She met Torvin at a smuggler's den in the Wailing Scar, where the boundaries between timelines grew thin. He was a cartographer's apprentice with ink-stained fingers and a desperate need to reach a woman named Messa on the other side of reality's fracture. "I can guide you," Raen said, and meant something deeper: I can love you across impossible distances. They traveled together through the Wailing Scar's shifting geometry. In the markets that existed in three places at once, they bartered fragments of memory for passage. In the inns that phased between versions, they learned to hold each other in the moments when their bodies both occupied the same timeline. Torvin mapped the routes they traveled; Raen sang the old merchant-songs that opened doors between iterations.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Last Hymn
stories.text: Naela had carved consciousness-fragments into bone reliquaries for forty years, each note of the Elder Tongue costing her a memory. By the Remaking's eve, she could no longer recall her own mother's face. The great working was set to begin at the Singing Cathedrals beneath the Sunken Marches—a ritual to consolidate all scattered fragments into a single Primal Shard that might anchor the fractured world. The bone-singers gathered: Torval with fingers bleeding from carving; young Moriel, barely past her initiation; Old Vrenn, who'd lost his eyes to the frequency-songs years ago. Naela carried the master bone-flute, carved from the spine of a creature that predated kingdoms. When she raised it to her lips, the entire cathedral began to sing. The fragments responded, their consciousness-light drawing together, converging on the Primal Shard hidden in the vault below. But something went wrong. The frequency didn't settle; it spiraled, hungry. Naela felt it trying to consume her—not her body, but her purpose, her reason for living. She could surrender and become part of the Shard, achieving a kind of transcendence.
